blurb: CSU's go-to red currant — pendant strigs of glossy, translucent ruby berries that glow on the bush and cook into jewel-bright jelly.
water: Moderate; needs steady summer moisture for good fruit size
spread: Clumping multi-cane shrub; does not run or self-sow
seasons: Greenish-yellow flower racemes Apr–May; translucent red berry strigs ripen in July; green summer foliage; bare cane frame in winter
wildlife: Bees work the spring flowers; songbirds eat the berries if left unnetted
habitat: Damp woods and stream margins on fertile soils

care.hardiness: USDA zones 3–7 — fully hardy across the Front Range (5b–6a) and grown in Colorado up to ~10,000 ft elevation. Red currants actually prefer cool summers, so they reward an east-facing or lightly shaded site over a hot, baking exposure.[1][2]
care.planting: Set out bare-root or container plants in early spring while still dormant, placing the crown 1–2 in below the soil line so new canes form from the base. Early fall (September) also works for container stock, giving roots time to settle before hard freeze. Currants can be grown a season or two in a large container, but pots dry out fast in Boulder's wind and sun and the roots freeze far harder than zone-3 wood does in open ground — sink the pot, heap mulch around it, or move it to an unheated garage over winter; plan to move it into the ground for best cropping.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun to part shade. Red currant is one of the few fruit shrubs that genuinely tolerates part shade — it fruits best in full sun but still crops well in light or afternoon shade, which is welcome on the hot Front Range where afternoon shade reduces heat and water stress.[1][2]
care.soil: Prefers fertile, organically rich, well-drained loam at a pH of 6 to 7, but tolerates Boulder's heavier alkaline clay at higher pH — amend clay with compost to improve drainage and moisture retention. It is hardy and crops even on poorer soils.[1][2]
care.water: Apply adequate, steady irrigation through the growing season for best fruit size and yield; the shallow roots dislike both drought and waterlogging. After harvest, gradually reduce water to harden the plant for winter, then give a deep watering in November before the ground freezes.[1]
care.spacing: Space plants 3.5–4.5 ft apart, in rows 6–8 ft apart if you grow more than one. Each bush matures to about 3–5 ft tall and wide.[1][2]
care.propagation: Easiest from hardwood cuttings of year-old wood taken in late fall to winter (8 in lengths) or by mound layering — heap soil over the base of the bush in spring and detach the rooted shoots the following year. Seed is not used for named cultivars (it won't come true). 'Red Lake' is self-fertile, so a single bush sets fruit; one variety is all you need.[1][3][2]
care.bloom: Inconspicuous greenish-yellow saucer flowers hang in short drooping racemes in April–May; bees and other early pollinators work them. The berries that follow ripen in July.[2][1]
care.feeding: Currants are light feeders. A spring dressing of compost or well-composted manure, or a modest granular feed before bud-swell, is plenty; on good garden soil little is needed. Avoid heavy nitrogen, which pushes soft growth at the expense of fruit.[1][3]
care.maintenance: Red currants fruit on spurs of two- and three-year-old wood, so prune in late winter or early spring before the buds swell. Aim for an open bush of about three canes each of one-, two- and three-year-old wood; each year remove the oldest stems (over three years old) at the base and thin crowded younger growth. This keeps the bush productive and airy, which also limits powdery mildew.[1][3]
care.harvest: Pick whole strigs (clusters) when the berries are fully red and translucent, usually over a two-to-three-week window in July. They hang well on the bush once ripe. Net against birds if you want the crop yourself.[1][2]
care.troubles: On the Front Range pests are minor — watch for aphids (which curl new leaves), cane borers, and spider mites, none of which usually need spraying. Powdery mildew can appear on crowded bushes; prune for airflow and avoid overhead watering. Like all Ribes, red currant is an alternate host for white pine blister rust (Cronartium ribicola), but it is a far weaker host than black currant and Colorado has no statewide ban; as a courtesy, avoid planting right beside susceptible five-needle (white) pines.[1][3]
care.companions: Tucks neatly into a part-shade corner of an edible garden or the dappled edge of a shrub border, underplanted with low herbs and spring bulbs. Keep it clear of five-needle white pines (a blister-rust precaution). It pairs naturally with the other Ribes — black currant and gooseberry — in a small-fruit bed.[1][3]

edible.summary: The translucent red berries are edible and excellent — tart fresh, superb cooked into jelly, syrup, and sauce; no part of the plant is poisonous.[1][2]
edible.parts: The fruit is the part eaten: small, glossy, translucent red berries borne in pendant clusters (strigs), ripening in July. They are pleasantly tart fresh and famously good cooked, being naturally high in pectin so they set into bright jelly, jam, syrup, cordial, and sauce with ease. The leaves, stems, and roots are not used as food.[1][2]
edible.preparation: Eat fully ripe berries fresh out of hand or strip them from the strigs for cooking. They are most often cooked with sugar into jelly, jam, syrup, and sauce, or added to summer puddings and baked goods; their high pectin also helps set lower-pectin fruits. Wash before use and pick out stems and any leaves.[1][2]
edible.caution: Red currant has no toxic parts — the berries, leaves, and stems are not poisonous, so it is safe to grow around children and pets. The only real cautions are ordinary ones: the raw fruit is quite tart, so most people prefer it cooked and sweetened, and as with any garden fruit, eat only berries from plants you have not sprayed with pesticides. Note that the bush is an alternate host for white pine blister rust, a tree disease — that is a concern for nearby white pines, not for eating the fruit.[3][1]
